narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the resilience it takes to get here with your upper-middle-class family background and commitment to full-time studies. The admissions model we use looks at patterns from past students to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated your risk of failure at about 51%, which tipped the decision against your application right now. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or spirit—it's based on how your profile stacks up against thousands of others who've gone through law school and the bar. The biggest factor working in your favor was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which sat right around the middle of the pack compared to most applicants and strongly suggested you'd perform solidly toward the end of undergrad. However, your LSAT score, which was noticeably below the typical 36.7 average, played a major role in pushing the prediction toward failure risk. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.0, lower than the dataset's average of 3.21, added to that concern by signaling a bit less academic edge than what the model sees in students who typically pass. Your family's upper-middle income level also nudged things slightly toward higher risk, as patterns show it doesn't always correlate with bar success in the same way for everyone. Your Year 1 decile in the middle range had a smaller but still concerning pull toward failure odds, while your full-time study plan offered a minor boost. Overall, compared to typical students who get the green light, your scores and grades were a step below the stronger profiles that predict bar passage. This doesn't define you—many overcome similar hurdles with targeted prep. Next, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and seeking mentorship from Black women lawyers who've navigated this path successfully. You're capable of turning this around; keep pushing forward.
